short.headline: India's borrowing map has moved from industry-first to personal, services, gold and finance-linked credit.
short.dek: RBI/IDH gives the granular bank-credit map. BIS and IMF add the wider household-debt and credit-system context without replacing the Indian loan-product data.
short.body: The richer source changes the article. IndiaDataHub exposes RBI Banking/Credit monthly series in both old and current classifications, so most major lines can be read from April 2007 to April 2026 with a visible taxonomy break at January 2019. The expanded artifact now covers 22,531 observations across 88 grouped monthly line items. Over the long bridge, personal loans rose from Rs 4.7 lakh crore in April 2007 to Rs 69.6 lakh crore in April 2026. Services rose to Rs 59.5 lakh crore and industry to Rs 45.4 lakh crore. Within personal credit, housing is still the anchor, but gold loans are now Rs 4.9 lakh crore and grew about 20x between January 2019 and April 2026.

macha.heading: India is not just borrowing more. It is borrowing through more kinds of credit.
macha.body: The old mental model was bank lending equals industry. That is too narrow. Banks still finance infrastructure, power, metals and companies, but the faster-moving map is homes, other personal loans, vehicles, cards, gold, NBFCs, trade, real estate, MSEs and priority-sector credit.
macha.soWhat: Read this as outstanding bank credit: a map of where loans sit on bank balance sheets, not fresh disbursals, default risk, or household stress.

article.title: How India borrows
article.standfirst: The long monthly evidence says India's bank-credit book has shifted from an industry-first story to a personal-and-services story. Homes remain the base, but gold, cards, vehicles, NBFCs, trade, priority-sector lending and infrastructure all matter. BIS and IMF add the wider debt context: India is changing how it borrows, but it is not simply the world's most household-indebted economy.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## Where non-food bank credit went

Start with the broad map. In April 2007, personal loans were Rs 4.7 lakh crore, services were Rs 4 lakh crore, and industry was Rs 6.7 lakh crore. By April 2026, personal loans were Rs 69.6 lakh crore, services were Rs 59.5 lakh crore, industry was Rs 45.4 lakh crore, and agriculture was Rs 26.3 lakh crore.

That is the central answer. India's bank-credit book has become more personal and more services-heavy. Industry did not disappear; it rose in absolute terms. But it no longer carries the whole story. The useful question is not only whether credit rose. It is where that credit now sits.

## The latest credit mix

The latest current-classification month in the IDH/RBI sectoral series is April 2026. The order is now personal loans first, services second, industry third, agriculture fourth. That ordering is a balance-sheet fact, not a mood.

Personal loans are therefore not a side pocket in the banking system. Services are also not a small residual category. Together they explain why the old shorthand of bank credit as mainly industrial credit is no longer enough.

## What is inside personal loans?

Personal loans are not one product. Housing or mortgage loans are the anchor at Rs 33.5 lakh crore in April 2026. Other personal loans are Rs 17.3 lakh crore. Vehicle loans are Rs 7.4 lakh crore. Gold loans are Rs 4.9 lakh crore. Credit-card outstanding is Rs 3 lakh crore. Loans against fixed deposits and education loans are each about Rs 1.6 lakh crore.

This is the most important clarification in the article. Housing is still the base, but the personal-credit story is not just homes. It includes unsecured or less clearly labelled other personal loans, vehicle finance, card balances, gold-backed borrowing, education loans and loans against existing assets.

## Which personal loans grew fastest?

From April 2007 to April 2026, total personal loans rose about 14.9x. Other personal loans rose about 20.7x. Credit-card outstanding rose about 15.1x. Housing or mortgage loans rose about 14.4x. Vehicle loans rose about 12.2x.

That long-history comparison is only for lines where the old-classification monthly history exists. It says the retail book widened beyond housing. It does not include gold, because the IDH probe did not find a clean old-classification monthly gold-loan pair.

## The personal-loan engine

The line chart shows the path behind the ranking. Housing builds steadily. Other personal loans become the second large retail bucket. Vehicles and cards become visible but smaller channels. Education remains much smaller.

Read this as outstanding stock, not monthly disbursement. A rising line means more credit remains on bank balance sheets at the reporting date. It can reflect new borrowing, older loans not yet repaid, longer loan tenors, formalisation, or classification changes.

## Since 2019, gold lending grew fastest

Gold needs its own window because the comparable monthly data starts in the current classification. From January 2019 to April 2026, bank gold loans rose from about Rs 0.24 lakh crore to Rs 4.9 lakh crore, about 20x. Over the same window, credit-card outstanding rose about 3.6x, vehicle loans about 2.6x, other personal loans about 3.3x, and housing about 2.9x.

The 20x number should not be read like the 2007-to-2026 chart. It starts from a smaller base and uses a shorter window. But it is still a major signal: gold is the fastest-growing named retail product in the comparable current-classification period.

## Gold became a separate retail-credit story

The rupee level now matters as much as the growth multiple. By April 2026, bank gold-loan outstanding was larger than credit-card outstanding and education loans. It was no longer a tiny line hidden at the edge of personal credit.

That does not mean every rupee is household distress. Gold-loan balances can rise because more households borrow, because banks formalise lending that used to sit outside banks, because gold prices raise collateral capacity, or because reporting improves. But gold clearly belongs in the story of how India borrows.

## Bank data is not the whole household-debt story

The RBI/IDH charts above are the right source for Indian bank-credit composition. They tell us homes, other personal loans, vehicles, gold, cards and education. BIS Total Credit answers a different question: how much household credit exists across the whole credit system?

On the BIS measure, credit to Indian households and NPISHs from all sectors was 45.5% of GDP in 2025-Q3. In domestic-currency terms, that is about Rs 150.6 lakh crore. That is much larger than the Rs 69.6 lakh crore of bank personal loans in the April 2026 IDH/RBI table because the definitions, lender universe and dates differ.

This is why BIS is useful but not a replacement. It gives scale and a denominator. It does not break Indian household credit into housing, gold, cards, vehicles or education. For those product lines, RBI/IDH remains the source.

## Is India highly household-indebted?

Against peers, India is not at the top of the household-debt table. IMF Global Debt Database puts India's household debt at 40.8% of GDP in 2024. Canada is about 100.1%, Korea 90.1%, Thailand 86.7% in its latest 2023 point, the UK 76.2%, the US 69.4%, China 61.4%, Germany 49.9%, Brazil 36.4%, South Africa 33.7%, Mexico 16.7%, and Indonesia 16.2%.

So the article should not say simply that India is uniquely over-borrowed. The sharper point is compositional: India's bank book has changed fast, especially toward personal credit, gold loans and finance-linked services. The macro household-debt ratio is still moderate compared with many richer and East Asian economies.

## Banks are not the whole credit system

BIS also shows why bank-credit charts need a boundary line. In 2025-Q3, India's private non-financial credit from all lenders was 97.4% of GDP. The domestic-bank slice was 59.5% of GDP. Banks are central, but they are not the entire credit system.

This matters for services credit too. When banks lend to NBFCs, a loan can sit in the bank-credit table as services or finance-company credit while the end borrower may be a household or business elsewhere. The RBI table shows the bank's immediate borrower category. It does not always show the final household or firm using the money.

## Is credit running hot?

The BIS credit-to-GDP gap adds a risk lens. It compares private non-financial credit-to-GDP with a backward-looking long-run trend. For India, the latest BIS gap is -3.1 percentage points in 2025-Q3: actual private non-financial credit is 97.4% of GDP against a BIS trend of 100.5%.

That is not a clean bill of health, and it is not a forecast. But it keeps the interpretation disciplined. The visible Indian story here is not just an economy-wide credit boom. It is a change in where credit sits: retail products, gold collateral, NBFCs, trade, priority-sector categories and infrastructure.

## Services credit is finance-heavy

Services credit is not just restaurants, software and transport. In April 2026, NBFCs alone had Rs 20.6 lakh crore of outstanding bank credit. Trade had Rs 13.2 lakh crore. Other services had Rs 12.2 lakh crore. Commercial real estate had Rs 6.4 lakh crore. HFCs and PFIs add more finance-adjacent credit.

That makes services a layered credit bucket. Some of it is ordinary business credit. Some of it is credit to financial intermediaries that may lend onward. Some of it sits around trade and real estate. Services credit is not the same thing as household borrowing, but it is part of the same shift away from the old industry-only shorthand.

## Where services credit went

The history of services credit is led by finance-company and trade credit, not by a simple catch-all service economy story. NBFC credit is especially important because banks lending to NBFCs can later become credit to households, small firms or other borrowers outside this table's immediate classification.

That is why services needs to be read carefully. It is a borrower-sector label on a bank balance sheet. It may point to onward lending, working capital, trade finance, commercial real estate, or direct services activity depending on the sub-line.

## Priority-sector credit adds another lens

Priority-sector lending is a policy lens, not a clean borrower-sector lens. In April 2026, the priority-sector table shows micro and small enterprises at Rs 29.7 lakh crore, agriculture at Rs 26.2 lakh crore, weaker sections at Rs 19.8 lakh crore, housing at Rs 10.4 lakh crore, and medium enterprises at Rs 7.1 lakh crore.

Do not add those numbers to the non-food sectoral buckets above. The point is different: Indian bank credit is shaped not only by demand for loans, but also by directed-lending categories that push banks toward agriculture, small enterprises, housing and weaker-section lending.

## Which industries borrow most?

Industry still matters, and the latest industry-detail map is led by infrastructure at Rs 14.9 lakh crore. Power is Rs 8.5 lakh crore. Basic metals are Rs 5.1 lakh crore, iron and steel Rs 3.5 lakh crore, roads Rs 3.4 lakh crore, engineering Rs 3.1 lakh crore, chemicals Rs 3.1 lakh crore, textiles Rs 3 lakh crore, and gems and jewellery Rs 1.2 lakh crore.

The point is balance. The industrial credit book is still large, capital-intensive and concentrated. But the broad industry bucket is now smaller than personal loans and services in the April 2026 sectoral split.

## Infrastructure still anchors industrial credit

Inside industry, infrastructure is the line to watch. It is much larger than most other industrial categories because infrastructure projects are capital-intensive and debt-heavy. Power and roads sit inside that broader infrastructure story, so the industry charts should be read as a hierarchy, not as a list to blindly sum.

Read this as borrower-industry credit outstanding, not as a measure of output, project quality, profitability or whether the borrowing is healthy. The chart tells you where bank credit is parked, not whether every rupee was well used.

## The industrial long tail

Once infrastructure is separated out, there is no single clean second industrial story. Engineering, chemicals, textiles, food processing, construction, transport equipment and gems and jewellery each form mid-sized credit pockets.

This long tail is useful because it keeps industry from becoming only an infrastructure chart. Industrial borrowing is still a cluster of many activities, some tied to domestic demand, some to exports, some to construction cycles, and some to commodity prices.

## What changed in the data itself?

This article uses IndiaDataHub as the access layer because it exposes RBI Banking/Credit identifiers directly. The expanded pull fetches all grouped monthly lines found in the probe: 88 line items and 188 series variants, producing 22,531 observations. Many line items have old-classification monthly series from April 2007 and current monthly series from January 2019. The chart builder uses old-classification data through December 2018 and current-classification data from January 2019.

BIS and IMF are added only for macro context. BIS Total Credit and BIS Credit-to-GDP Gaps are quarterly cross-country credit-system datasets. IMF Global Debt Database HH_LS is annual household debt by loans and debt securities as a share of GDP. These sources are excellent for scale, peer comparison and risk context; they are not the source for India's loan-product taxonomy.

That splice is useful, but it is not magic. January 2019 is a taxonomy boundary, not just a month. RBI also notes later format and reporting changes, including a revised format from January 2021, merger effects from July 2023, a gold-loan classification note from May 2024, and a reporting-fortnight definition change from December 31, 2025.

## What should a reader take away?

The short version is: India's banks are lending into a different economy than the one implied by the old industry-first shorthand. The credit book now has a large household-facing side, a large services-and-finance side, a policy-shaped priority-sector side, and a still-important industrial-infrastructure side.

That is why the composition matters. A rupee of bank credit can be a home loan, a gold loan, a vehicle loan, a card balance, an NBFC loan, trade finance, commercial real estate credit, an MSE loan, a road project, a power loan or gems-and-jewellery credit. The story changes depending on which line is growing.

editorialStory.title: India is not just borrowing more. It is borrowing differently.
editorialStory.dek: The old bank-credit story was industry, projects and infrastructure. The new one has homes, other personal loans, gold, NBFCs, trade, policy lending and still a lot of infrastructure.
editorialStory.lede: This is not a morality tale about Indians suddenly becoming reckless borrowers. It is a map of where credit now sits. The data says the banking system has moved toward household-facing credit and finance-linked services, while industry remains large but more concentrated.

numberCards.caption: Largest selected non-food bank-credit bucket in Apr 2026

numberCards.label: Gold loans
numberCards.caption: Now larger than credit-card outstanding and education loans

numberCards.label: Household credit, all lenders

pullQuotes.quote: The big shift is not simply more debt. It is a different credit map.
pullQuotes.context: RBI/IDH for loan composition; BIS/IMF for the wider credit-system context

pullQuotes.quote: Gold is no longer a footnote in the bank book.

jargon.plainMeaning: The amount still owed at the reporting date.
jargon.whyItMattersHere: These charts show loan stocks, not fresh loans issued that month.
It is the standard RBI lens for sectoral bank lending.
Banks lend to NBFCs, and NBFCs may then lend onward to households or businesses.

jargon.plainMeaning: Loans banks are directed to make to policy-priority areas such as agriculture, small businesses, housing and weaker sections.
jargon.whyItMattersHere: It is a policy category, not a clean borrower-sector category.

jargon.plainMeaning: The difference between credit as a share of GDP and a long-run trend line.
jargon.whyItMattersHere: It is a risk signal, not a product-composition chart.

sections.heading: The bank book has moved toward people and services
body: In 2007, the easy mental model was that bank credit meant industry. By 2026, that model breaks. Personal loans are Rs 69.6 lakh crore, services are Rs 59.5 lakh crore, and industry is Rs 45.4 lakh crore.
Industry still matters. But it is no longer the whole story. The credit map now has a large household-facing side and a large services-and-finance side.

sections.heading: Personal loans are not one thing
body: Housing is still the anchor. But the personal-loan bucket also contains other personal loans, vehicles, gold, cards, education and loans against existing assets.
That matters because each product says something different. A home loan is not a card balance. A gold loan is not an education loan. Treating all of them as one blob hides the real story.
sections.pullQuote: If you say 'personal loans' without opening the box, you miss the article.

sections.heading: Gold moved from cultural asset to visible bank-credit line
body: Gold loans grew about 20x from January 2019 to April 2026 in the current RBI classification. That does not automatically mean distress. It can also reflect formalisation, gold prices, better reporting and banks becoming more active in this market.
But the size is now impossible to ignore. At Rs 4.9 lakh crore, bank gold-loan outstanding is larger than credit-card outstanding and education loans.
sections.pullQuote: Gold is collateral, liquidity and emergency borrowing capacity. In India, that is not a side note.

sections.heading: The RBI tables show bank books. BIS shows the wider credit system.
body: RBI/IDH is the right source for Indian loan products. BIS is the right source for the wider credit-system denominator. On the BIS measure, household credit from all sectors was 45.5% of GDP in 2025-Q3.
That does not replace the RBI product split. BIS does not tell us how much of household credit is housing, gold or cards. It tells us the broader scale.
numberLabels: BIS household credit to GDP, latest
IMF household debt to GDP, latest

sections.heading: Services credit is finance-heavy
body: Services credit is not just restaurants, hotels and software firms. NBFCs alone had Rs 20.6 lakh crore of outstanding bank credit in April 2026.

That means some bank lending to 'services' may be lending to finance companies that lend onward. The bank table tells you the immediate borrower; it does not always tell you the final user of the money.

sections.heading: Industry is smaller in the broad split, but infrastructure still anchors it
body: The industrial story is not dead. It is concentrated. Infrastructure credit is Rs 14.9 lakh crore, with power, roads, metals and engineering forming the main industrial credit spine.
So the right reading is not 'India stopped borrowing for industry.' It is: industry is no longer the only credit story, and inside industry the debt-heavy infrastructure lines still dominate.

sections.heading: The cleaner conclusion
body: India's credit landscape has changed in composition more than it has become a simple overheating story. The BIS credit-to-GDP gap is negative in the latest data, which argues against calling this a broad credit boom.
The better conclusion is more specific: bank credit has become more personal, more gold-linked, more finance-intermediated, more policy-shaped and still meaningfully infrastructure-heavy.

editorialPlan.audience: Indian readers who want a richer, historically grounded map of bank-credit composition.
editorialPlan.heroDescription: A long-history monthly line chart using month-level x positions, showing personal loans, services, industry and agriculture from April 2007 to April 2026, with the old/current classification boundary noted.

selectedDataPoints.label: Long monthly personal-loan history
selectedDataPoints.reason: It shows the retail-credit shift before the 2019 SIBC window.

selectedDataPoints.label: Personal-loan subcomponents
selectedDataPoints.reason: The article needs to explain the inside of personal credit, not just the total.

selectedDataPoints.label: IndiaDataHub old/current classification bridge
selectedDataPoints.reason: The history is useful only if the taxonomy break is visible.

pullQuotes.quote: Personal loans rose from Rs 4.7 lakh crore in April 2007 to Rs 69.6 lakh crore in April 2026.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The amount still owed to banks at the reporting date.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Every chart is a stock of credit outstanding, not fresh loans issued that month.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Bank credit excluding food credit, commonly used for sectoral credit analysis.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The broad buckets in this article are non-food credit categories.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: RBI/IDH line items before the 2019 sectoral-credit format change.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It extends history back to 2007, but it should not be treated as a perfectly unchanged taxonomy.

chartExplainers.title: Where non-food bank credit went
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It answers the article question over the longest monthly window available from the IDH/RBI bridge.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Read each line as outstanding INR crore. The pre-2019 part is old classification; the post-2019 part is current classification.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not treat the 2019 boundary as a normal month-to-month kink.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Keep four lines and label latest values.

chartExplainers.title: The latest credit mix
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It gives the current balance across the broad credit buckets.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Segments are latest current-classification outstanding amounts.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read this as all bank assets or as household debt only.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a compact stacked strip with labels.

chartExplainers.title: What is inside personal loans?
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It breaks the largest bucket into products readers can understand, including gold.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Bars rank latest outstanding credit by personal-loan subcategory.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Credit-card outstanding is not card transaction volume; gold has a shorter monthly history.

chartExplainers.title: Which personal loans grew fastest?
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It separates current size from long-run growth for the lines with 2007 old-classification history.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each bar is latest outstanding divided by April 2007 outstanding.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Gold is excluded because there is no old-classification monthly pair in the probe.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use one decimal place and an x suffix.

chartExplainers.title: The personal-loan engine
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It shows how housing, other personal, vehicles, cards and education moved over time.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each line is an outstanding-stock series in INR crore.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read outstanding stock as monthly borrowing.

chartExplainers.title: Since 2019, gold lending grew fastest
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It gives gold its proper place using the comparable current-classification window.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each bar is April 2026 outstanding divided by January 2019 outstanding.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not compare this 2019-window chart with the 2007-window chart as if they use the same base.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Put gold first and show the base window.

chartExplainers.title: Gold became a separate retail-credit story
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It shows gold against other smaller personal-loan products after the current taxonomy begins.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Lines are current-classification outstanding stocks from January 2019 onward.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: A rising balance is not automatically distress; collateral values and formalisation can matter.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Keep gold, cards, vehicles and education visible.

chartExplainers.title: Services credit is finance-heavy
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It shows that services credit includes finance companies, trade and real estate.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Bars are latest outstanding credit for selected services subcategories.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: NBFC credit is not direct household borrowing.

chartExplainers.title: Where services credit went
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It shows the path of the services sub-buckets, not just the latest ranking.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each line is outstanding bank credit to a services subcategory.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not add trade to wholesale and retail trade unless the taxonomy says they are separate in that view.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use end labels and avoid too many thin lines.

chartExplainers.title: Priority-sector credit is not one thing
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It adds the policy-lending lens that shapes Indian bank credit.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Bars are latest priority-sector outstanding amounts by named line item.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Priority-sector rows are a separate classification lens and should not be added to sectoral rows.

chartExplainers.title: Which industries borrow most?
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It gives the current industrial-credit map, including power, roads, steel and gems and jewellery.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Bars are selected industry-detail categories, not the broad industry total.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not add child rows to parent rows.

chartExplainers.title: Infrastructure still anchors industrial credit
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It shows the industrial borrower history behind the latest ranking.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each line is outstanding credit to a selected industry-detail category.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This is borrower industry, not project quality or profitability.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use selected lines and latest labels.

chartExplainers.title: The industrial long tail
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It prevents the industry section from becoming only infrastructure.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each line is outstanding credit to a selected mid-sized industry category.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Compare levels and movement; do not sum parent and child categories.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Prefer latest bars on very small screens.

sectionVisualMap.heading: Gold became a separate retail-credit story
Priority-sector credit is not one thing

sourceNotes: Primary access layer: IndiaDataHub Economic Monitor, Banking/Credit category, RBI-sourced monthly identifiers.
Expanded normalized artifact: data/series/indiadatahub.IN.banking.idh.credit_monthly.long.json, 22,531 rows across 88 grouped monthly line items and 188 fetched series variants.

BIS Total Credit artifact: data/series/bis.IN.credit.total_credit_quarterly.long.json, selected quarterly total-credit rows for household, private non-financial, government and related borrower-sector metrics.
BIS Credit-to-GDP Gaps artifact: data/series/bis.IN.credit.credit_gap_quarterly.long.json, selected quarterly private non-financial credit-to-GDP actual, trend and gap rows.
IMF Global Debt Database artifact: data/series/imf.GDD.HH_LS.household_debt_pct_gdp.long.json, annual household debt by loans and debt securities as percent of GDP.
RBI SIBC Excel archive remains a cross-check for latest line items and taxonomy notes.
caveats: The long monthly charts splice old-classification series through December 2018 with current-classification series from January 2019; this extends history but is not a perfectly unchanged taxonomy.
Gold loans have current monthly coverage from January 2019 in the IDH probe, but no old-classification monthly pair was found, so gold is charted in the current-classification window.
BIS Total Credit has a household borrower sector for India, but it does not provide a household product breakup for India; housing, gold, cards, vehicles and education remain RBI/IDH product lines.
IMF GDD HH_LS is annual and cross-country. Use it for peer context, not Indian bank-credit composition.
BIS credit-to-GDP gaps are an early-warning/risk lens based on a one-sided HP-filter trend, not a direct forecast or product-level loan measure.
RBI notes matter: January 2019 and January 2021 format changes, July 2023 merger effects, a May 2024 gold-loan classification note, and a December 31, 2025 reporting-fortnight definition change.
These are outstanding credit stocks, not new loans issued during the month.
This is aggregate bank-credit data; it cannot identify borrower income, region, caste, gender, defaults or household repayment stress.
Some industry and services rows are parent-child categories; charts compare selected lines and should not be blindly summed.
